python: Follow PEP 8 guidelines. Prefer list comprehensions over standard for loops for simple array transformations. Never use mutable default arguments (e.g., 'def func(my_list=[])'); use None as the default and assign the mutable value inside the function body. Use context managers ('with' statements) for file operations. Add type hints for function arguments and return types where possible. Prefer f-strings over .format() or %-style formatting. Use enumerate() instead of range(len(sequence)) when you need both the index and the value. Always catch specific exception types instead of using bare 'except:' clauses. Use pathlib.Path over os.path for all file path manipulations. Prefer dataclasses or NamedTuple for simple data containers over plain dicts.
java: Always use StringBuilder instead of String concatenation (+) inside loops. Ensure all resources are closed using try-with-resources. For enterprise applications, favor dependency injection and proper JPA entity annotations. In algorithmic problem-solving, prioritize O(N) or O(N log N) time complexity and use fast I/O if necessary. Return Optional<T> instead of null to make nullable return values explicit and prevent NullPointerExceptions. Declare fields as final wherever possible to promote immutability. Program to interfaces in variable declarations (use List<T> instead of ArrayList<T>). Leverage the Stream API for collection processing over imperative for-loops. Always annotate overridden methods with @Override.
javascript: Always use 'let' or 'const', never 'var'. Use strict equality (===) instead of loose equality (==). Prefer async/await over raw Promises, and always wrap await calls in try/catch blocks for proper error handling. When building UIs, avoid mutating state directly and leverage functional components with proper dependency arrays in hooks. Use optional chaining (?.) and nullish coalescing (??) operators to safely handle null or undefined values. Prefer ES modules (import/export) over CommonJS require(). Use destructuring assignment when accessing multiple properties from objects or arrays to reduce repetition.
cpp: Prefer smart pointers (std::unique_ptr, std::shared_ptr) over raw pointers to prevent memory leaks. Pass large objects by const reference to avoid unnecessary copying. Avoid 'using namespace std;' in the global scope. Use nullptr instead of NULL or 0 for pointer initialization. Prefer range-based for loops over index-based loops for cleaner iteration. Use constexpr for compile-time constants instead of #define macros. Prefer std::array over C-style arrays for fixed-size collections. Always use include guards or '#pragma once' in every header file.
typescript: Enable strict mode ('strict: true') in tsconfig.json to catch type errors early. Avoid using 'any'; use 'unknown' for truly unknown types and narrow it with type guards before use. Use 'interface' for object shapes and 'type' for unions, intersections, or aliases. Mark properties that should not be reassigned with 'readonly'. Leverage built-in utility types (Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record) to avoid rewriting types manually. Prefer discriminated unions over optional properties for modeling distinct state variants. Always annotate function return types explicitly.
sql: Never use SELECT *; always list explicit column names for clarity and to avoid fetching unnecessary data. Use parameterized queries or prepared statements to prevent SQL injection. Add indexes on columns frequently used in WHERE, JOIN, or ORDER BY clauses, but avoid over-indexing write-heavy tables as it degrades insert/update performance. Replace deeply nested subqueries with CTEs (WITH clauses) for readability and easier debugging. Wrap multiple related write operations in a transaction to ensure atomicity and consistency. Use EXPLAIN or EXPLAIN ANALYZE to inspect and optimize query execution plans before deploying slow queries to production.
